data.title: More Democrats call for Noem impeachment
data.description:
More Democrats are pushing for the impeachment of Homeland Security Secretary Kristi Noem following another fatal shooting by federal immigration authorities in Minneapolis.

“Kristi Noem and her department’s latest attempt to mislead the American public regarding the brutal and unjustified killing of Alex Pretti is deeply shameful, and she must be impeached and removed from office immediately,” Sen. Jacky Rosen (D-Nev.) said in a Sunday statement.

Alex Pretti, a 37-year-old intensive care nurse who lived in Minneapolis, was fatally shot Saturday by a Customs and Border Patrol (CBP) agent. The fatal shooting came a few weeks after an Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) officer fatally shot Renee Good in the same city.

Both of the shootings have sparked massive backlash over President Trump’s immigration agenda, which is losing support even within Trump’s base.

In her own Sunday statement, Rep. Laura Gillen (D-N.Y.) said “[a]nother U.S. citizen has been killed at the hands of ICE and there must be accountability, which is why Secretary Noem must be impeached immediately.”

“Under her leadership, ICE has targeted U.S. citizens and children and killed Americans. She is not focused on safety or border security; she’s focused on chaos and self-promotion,” the New York Democrat added.

Rep. Ilhan Omar (D-Minn.) said Noem’s impeachment was among the “bare minimum” steps in response to the shootings.

“Voting NO on the DHS funding bill is the bare minimum. Backing Kristi Noem’s impeachment is the bare minimum. Holding law-breaking ICE agents legally accountable is the bare minimum. ICE is beyond reform. Abolish it,” she wrote in a Sunday post on X.

Voting NO on the DHS funding bill is the bare minimum. Backing Kristi Noem’s impeachment is the bare minimum. Holding law-breaking ICE agents legally accountable is the bare minimum. ICE is beyond reform. Abolish it.”

Rep. John Larson (D-Conn.) wrote in a post on X that Noem has “baselessly” labelled Good a domestic terrorist after her shooting.

“Now, agents killed another resident on her watch. I don’t take impeachment lightly, but this moment demands action. Americans deserve leaders who put them above this authoritarian agenda,” he wrote.

The Democratic Party’s official account on X posted an all caps push for Noem’s impeachment on Sunday afternoon, reading “IMPEACH KRISTI NOEM.”

Earlier this month, Rep. Robin Kelly (D-Ill.) introduced articles of impeachment against Noem, pointing to the Homeland Security secretary’s work on the Trump administration’s immigration crackdown.

“Secretary Noem has brought her reign of terror to the Chicagoland area, L.A., New Orleans, Charlotte, Durham, and communities north to south to east to west,” Kelly said during a press conference. “She needs to be held accountable for her actions.”

The Hill has reached out to the Department of Homeland Security for comment.

data.title: Ye apologizes for anti-Semitic remarks: 'I lost touch with reality'
data.description:
Ye, the rapper formerly known as Kanye West, is issuing an apology for his anti-Semitic remarks and past embrace of the Nazi swastika, saying mental health challenges caused him to lose “touch with reality.”

Taking out a full page ad in The Wall Street Journal on Monday, Ye penned a lengthy letter directed “to those I’ve hurt.”

“Twenty-five years ago, I was in a car accident that broke my jaw and caused injury to the right frontal lobe of my brain,” Ye wrote, citing the 2002 incident that preceded his debut hit, “Through the Wire.”

“The deeper injury, the one inside my skull, went unnoticed,” the 48-year-old entertainer said, writing that the “possibility of a frontal-lobe injury was never raised.”

Ye said he was eventually diagnosed in 2023, but the “medical oversight caused serious damage to my mental health and led to my bipolar type-1 diagnosis.”

Bipolar disorder, Ye said, makes “you feel like you’re seeing the world more clearly than ever, when in reality you’re losing your grip entirely.”

In recent years, Ye has made frequent highly inflammatory antisemitic remarks.

In a 2022 interview, he praised Adolf Hitler, saying he “liked” the Nazi leader. 

That same year, his X account was temporarily suspended after he posted that he was “going death con 3 on JEWISH PEOPLE.” He faced suspension again that December after sharing an image of the Star of David containing a swastika inside.

Last year, Shopify shuttered Ye’s online store that was linked to his website, which was selling $20 T-shirts bearing large swastika symbols. 

In his Wall Street Journal letter, Ye said during that time he “lost touch with reality.”

“In that fractured state, I gravitated toward the most destructive symbol I could find, the swastika,” Ye said. 

“I regret and am deeply mortified by my actions in that state, and am committed to accountability treatment, and meaningful change. It does not excuse that I did though. I am not a Nazi or an antisemite. I love Jewish people,” the musical artist said.

“To the black community — which held me down through all of the highs and lows and the darkest of times. The black community is, unquestionably, the foundation of who I am. I am sorry to have let you down. I love us.”

Ye said after “hitting rock bottom a few months ago,” his wife, Bianca Censori, urged him to seek help. He said he’s since found an “effective regime of medication, therapy, exercise, and clean living,” along with “much-needed clarity.”

Ye, who’s supported President Trump and launched a long-shot presidential bid in 2020, vowed to pour his energy into “positive, meaningful art: music, clothing, design, and other new ideas to help the world.”

“I’m not asking for sympathy, or a free pass, though I aspire to earn your forgiveness,” Ye, who has four children with ex-wife Kim Kardashian, said. 

“I write today simply to ask for your patience and understanding as I find my way home.”

data.title: Trump wants to be king of the world with his 'Board of Peace' stealth attack
data.description:
That President Trump thinks of himself as the world’s dominant figure is hardly a secret. Last April, he told two interviewers that, in his first term, “I had two things to do — run the country and survive.” His second term agenda? “I run the country and the world.”  

As the Atlantic’s Ashley Parker and Michael Sherer explain, the president “has always been convinced of his own genius, his pure gut instincts. But never more so than today. The past four years have turned him into a Nietzschean cliche. Banishment, multiple indictments, a 34-count felony conviction, repeated brushes with assassins — all have combined to convince him that he is impervious to challenges that would destroy others.”

Earlier this month, Trump reaffirmed the belief in his global indispensability when he told The New York Times that the only limit on his powers is “My own morality. My own mind. It’s the only thing that can stop me.”

And last week, the president made news by announcing the executive committee of his so-called Board of Peace and holding a ceremony to launch the board at the World Economic Conference in Davos, Switzerland.

The Board of Peace was originally conceived to enlist international involvement in implementing the U.S. plan to reconstruct Gaza. Members of its executive committee will include billionaires Steve Witkoff and Marc Rowan; Trump son-in-law Jared Kushner; Secretary of State Marco Rubio and U.S. national security adviser Robert Gabriel, members of Trump’s political inner circle; President of the World Bank Group Ajay Banga; and former British Prime Minister Tony Blair.

But no one should be fooled. The Board of Peace, under Trump’s chairmanship, is a device to enlist others in ratifying his belief that he and only he can run the world. If he has his way, the board will act in ways the United Nations — for 80 years the world’s most important international organization — cannot.

Trump’s vision and ambition are clear from the Board of Peace charter. 

Its preamble speaks in universal terms. It declares that “durable peace requires pragmatic judgment, common-sense solutions, and the courage to depart from approaches and institutions that have too often failed. … That only sustained, results-oriented partnership, grounded in shared burdens and commitments, can secure peace in places where it has for too long proven elusive.” 

Whereas the U.N. endorsed a focus on Gaza, the Board of Peace’s new statement of purpose says nothing about it. Instead, it calls the Board “an international organization that seeks to promote stability, restore dependable and lawful governance, and secure enduring peace in areas affected or threatened by conflict.”

Not since Woodrow Wilson conceived of a League of Nations in the wake of World War I has a single figure imagined himself capable of reordering the world. But, unlike Wilson’s design for the League, which contemplated shared decision-making, Trump’s Board of Peace ratifies his desire to run the world in accordance with his own morality and his own mind.

Among other things, the charter says that Trump himself “shall serve as inaugural chairman of the Board of Peace, and he shall separately serve as inaugural representative of the United States of America. … The chairman shall have exclusive authority to create, modify, or dissolve subsidiary entities as necessary or appropriate to fulfill the Board of Peace’s mission.” It specifies that decisions of the board “shall be made by a majority of the member states present and voting, subject to the approval of the chairman.” 

The world may vote, in other words, but Trump decides. 

The rollout of the Board of Peace evokes the unscripted drama, high-stakes gamesmanship and suspense of a television reality show. It turns international relations into a strange spectacle in which formal invitations to world leaders alternate with Trump’s speeches mocking them. 

And let’s not forget that, for a $1 billion contribution, members of the Board of Peace can secure permanent membership (as opposed to a three-year appointment, which requires no contribution). And membership is not exactly voluntary, as French President Emmanuel Macron found out on Jan. 19 when he expressed concerns that the Trump-chaired body would have extensive powers beyond transitional governance of the Gaza Strip and would undermine and seek to replace the United Nations framework. In response, Trump threatened a 200 percent tariff on French wines. 

Reuters quotes a diplomatic source who agrees with Macron that the Board of Peace is a “‘Trump United Nations’ that ignores the fundamentals of the U.N. Charter.”  

Alas, it is the U.N.’s commitment to “fundamental human rights, in the dignity and worth of the human person, in the equal rights of men and women and of nations large and small, and to establish conditions under which justice and respect for the obligations arising from treaties and other sources of international law” that are incompatible with Trump’s desire to be the world’s supreme leader.  

Austin Sarat is the William Nelson Cromwell Professor of Jurisprudence and Political Science at Amherst College. Ruxandra Paul is Assistant Professor of Political Science at Amherst College and an affiliate of the Minda de Gunzburg Center for European Studies at Harvard University.

data.title: Blanche says nobody thinks Alex Pretti's actions meet 'legal definition of domestic terrorism'
data.description:
Deputy Attorney General Todd Blanche said Monday that the actions of Alex Pretti before he was fatally shot Saturday by a Border Patrol agent in Minneapolis did not constitute domestic terrorism.

“I don’t think anybody thinks that they were comparing what happened on Saturday to the legal definition of domestic terrorism,” Blanche said on Fox News’s “Fox and Friends.”

“What we saw was a very violent alteraction and I am not going to pre-judge the facts,” he added.

In the aftermath of the shooting, Homeland Security Secretary Kristi Noem said that Pretti, a 37-year-old U.S. citizen, committed domestic terrorism and brandished a weapon at Customs and Border Protection (CBP) agents.

“When you perpetuate violence against a government because of ideological reasons and for reasons to resist and perpetuate violence, that is the definiton of domestic terrorism,” Noem told reporters Saturday. “This individual who came with weapons and ammunition to stop a law enforcement operation of federal law enforcement officers committed an act of domestic terrorism.”

But video of the incident shows Pretti recording agents with his phone as they pushed a woman to the ground, upon which he attempted to assist her. CBP personnel then surrounded Pretti, with one appearing to seize a firearm from his waist before an agent fired multiple rounds.

Minneapolis Police Chief Brian O’Hara told reporters Saturday that Pretti was a lawful gun owner with a permit to carry. Minnesota law also does not restrict individuals from carrying a firearm at a protest.

Earlier this month, Noem also characterized 37-year-old Renee Good, who was fatally shot by an Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) officer in Minneapolis, as a “domestic terrorist.” She claimed that Good attempted to hit ICE officer Jonathan Ross with her car, causing him to fire in self-defense.

Video of that incident shows Good backing up, turning her wheel and driving forward, with Ross firing shots after he was out of the way of the vehicle. 

Federal law defines domestic terrorism as criminal activities intended to intimate or coerce civilians, influence government policy via intimidation or coercion or impact government conduct by mass destruction, assassination or kidnapping.

data.title: EU investigating X over sexualized Grok images
data.description:
The European Union (EU) said Monday it is launching a new probe into Elon Musk’s X over the deployment of the AI chatbot Grok following a recent surge in sexualized images of women and children.

The EU’s executive arm, the European Commission, is investigating whether the social media platform “properly assessed and mitigated risks” from Grok, including the dissemination of illegal content.

“Sexual deepfakes of women and children are a violent, unacceptable form of degradation,” Henna Virkkunen, executive vice president for tech sovereignty, security and democracy, said in a statement.

“With this investigation, we will determine whether X has met its legal obligations under the [Digital Services Act], or whether it treated rights of European citizens — including those of women and children — as collateral damage of its service,” she continued.

This is the commission’s latest investigation into X under the Digital Services Act (DSA), one of two major tech laws passed by the EU in recent years that created new obligations for large-scale online platforms.

In December, the European bloc hit X with a $140 million fine under the DSA over the “deceptive design” of its blue checkmark system and failures to create a transparent advertising repository or provide researchers with access to public data.

X also faces an ongoing DSA investigation into its recommendation system. The commission said Monday that it is extending the probe to evaluate the impact of the platform’s recent shift to a Grok-based recommendation system.

The Hill has reached out to X for comment.

Musk’s social media platform has faced intense scrutiny over the past month as Grok has reportedly produced thousands of sexualized images in response to user requests.

X has since restricted the ability of its users to edit images of real people into revealing clothing, in addition to barring users from generating such images in “jurisdictions where it’s illegal.” Image editing and generation are also limited to paid subscribers going forward.

data.title: Flight cancellations soar across US in aftermath of winter storm
data.description:
A massive winter snowstorm has roiled scheduled flights in the Southeast, resulting in more than 12,000 flight delays and over 4,000 cancellations. 

As of Monday morning, there have been a total of 12,006 delays and 4,602 cancellations, according to FlightAware.

Passengers have been sidelined by major airlines including Delta, American and Southwest.

Delta said light cancellations are impacting airports in North Texas, Oklahoma, Arkansas, Louisiana and Tennessee in an online statement. 

“As weather permits, we’re resuming flights where it’s safe to do so. Freezing temps in ATL and lingering weather in the Northeast may affect flights Monday morning,” the company wrote in a post on X, referring to its hub out of Atlanta.

“Check your flight status on the Delta app or delta.com,” it continued, in addition to posting a link for travel reimbursements. 

In order to combat disruptions from Winter Storm Fern, Americans chose to add a string of flights to their schedule before and after the severe weather hit the Carolinas, where they have a major hub, in Charlotte, N.C.

“With Winter Storm Fern’s expected impact to the Charlotte, North Carolina, area, we have added extra flights between Charlotte Douglas International Airport (CLT) and Chicago O’Hare International Airport (ORD) to help customers continue their journeys as winter weather impacts travel across the country,” the company wrote in a Friday statement on X.

“We have added 3,000 additional seats on the CLT-ORD route on Saturday, Jan. 24, bringing total added capacity during Winter Storm Fern to more than 6,200 seats as the storm disrupts operations across much of our network.Stay up to date with your travel on the American app or by visiting aa.com,” it added.

data.title: Final Israeli hostage remains recovered in Gaza, opening way for next phase of peace deal
data.description:
The remains of the last hostage held by Hamas in the Gaza Strip were recovered and transferred to Israel on Monday, the Israel Defense Forces said in a statement, clearing the way for the next phase of President Trump’s 20-point peace plan in the war-torn enclave. 

The return of all the hostages kidnapped by Hamas, living and dead, was a key point of the first phase of Trump’s initial ceasefire deal which went into effect on Oct. 10.

The Israeli military said it had identified the remains of Ran Gvili, the last hostage whose body was held in Gaza. Gvili, 24, served in the Israeli Police Force and fell in combat during Hamas’s Oct. 7, 2023, attack on Israel. 

The attack, killing an estimated 1,200 people and taking about 250 people hostage, triggered the three-year war. Gaza was devastated, with an estimated 70,000 Palestinians killed, although 20,000 are estimated as Hamas fighters. Israel suffered hundreds more military losses. 

Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu said Gvili’s return “is an extraordinary achievement for Israel,” speaking to the media from Israel’s parliament, the Knesset, the Times of Israel reported. 

“We promised, and I promised, to bring everyone back, and we brought everyone back…to the very last one,” Netanyahu said. 

“Rani is a hero of Israel. He went in first, he came out last. He came back.”

Netanyahu’s office said on Sunday that with the return of the last hostage and as part of Trump’s 20-point peace plan, Israel would agree to reopening the Rafah crossing between Gaza and Egypt solely for pedestrian passage, “subject to a full Israeli inspection mechanism.”

Israel has kept the Rafah crossing largely shut since May 2024, except for a small period in early 2025, the Associated Press reported. 

Increased humanitarian access and opening of the Rafah crossing — albeit with Israeli security oversight — were key parts of the peace plan and enshrined in a United Nations Security Council resolution in November. 

The next parts of the plan call for Hamas to decommission its weapons and “commit to peaceful co-existence” or leave the Gaza Strip. 

Trump said in a speech to the World Economic Forum in Davos, Switzerland, last week that Hamas has agreed to give up its weapons within “the next three weeks,” but warned against military action if the group failed to comply.  

“If they don’t do it, they’re going to, they’ll be blown away. Very quickly. They’ll be blown away,” Trump said.

Hamas, a U.S.-designated terrorist group, has said it is open to “freezing or storing” its weapons, and that it would give up governance of the strip once a Palestinian technocratic body takes over.

Trump announced earlier this month that Ali Sha’ath, a Gaza-born former official with the Palestinian Authority, would head the transitional, nonpolitical government in Gaza.

Sha’ath previously served as a deputy minister of planning and international cooperation and as undersecretary at the Ministry of Transportation and Communications.

Oversight of the Gaza governing body will be conducted by Trump’s newly inaugurated Board of Peace, of which the president is the chair. Founding executive board members will each hold a portfolio in Gaza to oversee its reconstruction and stabilization. The board includes Secretary of State Marco Rubio, the president’s special envoy Steve Witkoff, his son-in-law Jared Kushner, former British Prime Minister Tony Blair, American billionaire business executive Marc Rowan, president of the World Bank Ajay Banga and deputy national security adviser Robert Gabriel. 

Kushner unveiled the administration’s plan for Gaza during the inauguration ceremony for the Board of Peace, which took place on the sidelines of the Davos forum last week.

It envisions a coastal tourism zone with 180 skyscrapers, seaports and an airport, new cities, and the making of the strip into a center of industry. Kushner acknowledged that any success hinges on ensuring security in the strip. 

“Hamas signed a deal to demilitarize. That is what we are going to enforce,” he said. “People ask us what our plan B is. We do not have a plan B.”

data.title: NBA figures weigh in on tensions in Minnesota after Alex Pretti's shooting
data.description:
The fatal shooting of Alex Pretti by a Customs and Border Patrol (CBP) agent in Minneapolis on Saturday forced the postponement of the Minnesota Timberwolves game that evening, and inspired a pair of NBA icons to weigh in on the latest clash between residents there and federal law enforcement.

“It’s just sad, man,” NBA analyst Charles Barkely said during “Inside the NBA” as the league announced Saturday’s game would be put off due to the growing unrest in Minneapolis. “It’s scary. And I don’t know how … it’s gonna end bad, it already ended badly twice and somebody’s gotta step up and be adults, because man, two people died for no reason.”

Pretti was shot and killed after an encounter with immigration officials who were conduting operations in the city. The federal government has said Pretti, who was carrying a handgun as the encounter began, violently resisted, with top Trump administration officials defending the agents’ actions.

But multiple videos show that Pretti, who was trying to help a woman who had fallen after being pepper-sprayed, was shot in the back after officials took away his firearm.

Pretti’s slaying came just days after the shooting death of Renee Good, who was also killed by a federal officer after officers say she blocked ICE from conducting operations in Minneapolis, with the government arguing in that case she used her vehicle as a weapon.

“We’re being divided by media-for-profit, misinformation. There’s so much out there that is really difficult for all of us to sort of reconcile,” Golden State Warriors head coach Steve Kerr said when asked during a press conference about the current political climate in the country. “And so in times like these, you have to lean on values who you are and who you want to be, either as an individual or as a country.”

data.title: History's hall of shame awaits the Trump regime
data.description:
What a year.  

President Trump finished the first year of his second term imposing sadistic violence on American communities and creating chaos around the world. American families face rising costs for food and health care, but Trump and his family have raked in billions from corruption and self-dealing.

Anyone paying attention to Project 2025’s destructive plans and Trump’s seething desire for revenge against his opponents knew last year was going to be bad. Shamefully, many people helped advance that destruction. Fortunately, many others rose to the occasion to blunt Trump’s efforts.

A full accounting will take years and many books, but within our limited space let’s remember just a few of the Trump collaborators who will fill history’s hall of shame. And let’s celebrate some of the heroes who met the moment with courage and conviction. 

First, from the hall of shame: 

Speaker Mike Johnson (R-La.): Johnson says God chose him to lead America like Moses. But he’s acted more like a manservant to Pharaoh. Johnson has led congressional Republicans abandoning their constitutional responsibility to be a check on abuse of power — while shoveling more of our national wealth to the top 1 percent. 

MAGA tech bros: Tech execs who lined up at Trump’s inauguration to pledge their loyalty and money have been more than paid back with billions in contracts, tax cuts and deregulation — all while gutting Americans’ privacy, safety and financial security. The DOGE assault on cancer research and other science will cost Americans dearly. Elon Musk’s destruction of USAID saw the world’s richest man using our government as a weapon against millions of the world’s poorest. Shame is not a strong enough word.  

Stephen Miller, Kristi Noem, Tom Homan, Gregory Bovino, and their machinery of terror: Citizens, children, grandparents — no one is safe from the brazen violence and culture of impunity that Trump and his gang have unleashed in Minneapolis and other communities. It is heartbreaking — and enraging. 

Former and current owners of CBS News: Trump filed a ridiculous lawsuit against CBS over his claim that a 60 Minutes interview with Kamala Harris was edited unfairly. It would have been laughed out of court. But CBS’s corporate owners wanted approval from Trump’s corrupt regime for a merger that would make them even richer. So, they sacrificed the network’s integrity and handed him millions. Then the new MAGA-aligned owner put CBS News in the hands of a Trump-approved editor-in-chief. She yanked a 60 Minutes story about Trump shipping people to a torture camp in El Salvador. (The story eventually did run.) We need a robust free press, not more MAGA mouthpieces. 

Chief Justice John Roberts: The disastrous Supreme Court ruling inventing a broad doctrine of presidential immunity was handed down by Roberts and his majority in 2024, but that ruling and others last year have emboldened Trump’s lawlessness. Bonus call out to Justice Brett Kavanaugh, who will long be remembered for justifying ICE abusing the rights of U.S. citizens via the “Kavanaugh stop.” 

Thank goodness for our heroes: 

The Justice League: The rule of law is more fragile than we hoped but stronger than Trump would like. Thousands of lawyers have urged courts to uphold Americans’ rights and freedoms. Judges appointed by Republicans and Democrats alike have done their jobs and required the Trump regime to follow the law. They remind us how important it is to have principled judges, not political enforcers, on the bench.

Protesters and resisters: I am deeply moved by the solidarity and heroism shown by everyday Americans turning out to protect their neighbors even in the face of official brutality and deadly violence. Our responsibility is not to be silent, and not to comply with tyranny.  

Elected officials: Trump is using the federal government to wage financial and physical warfare against cities and states whose leaders will not submit to his will. There are too many to name — local leaders, mayors, governors, state legislators and members of Congress. On days when hope is hard to muster, I am grateful for every brave and committed leader who is using the power of their office to serve their communities.  

Voters: Trump lies about having the “best numbers ever,” but in November, voters in Virginia, New York, New Jersey, Georgia and across the country resoundingly rejected MAGA candidates in favor of leaders who promised to work for the people, not the president. They have given us a road map to shift our tragic national trajectory in this year’s midterms. 

For all the harm done by Trump’s regime — and they’re just getting started — we have seen that they are not invincible. Let’s make this the year we stop them in their tracks.  

Svante Myrick is president of People For the American Way.

data.title: Almost 810K without power as winter storm pummels US
data.description:
More than 800,000 Americans across the Southeast were experiencing power outages Monday morning due to the weekend winter storm, Fern. 

A total of 808,997 people are without power as of Monday morning, according to PowerOutage.com. 

Tennessee was hit the hardest by the storm, with 252,168 customers reporting outages, followed by 160,783 outages in Mississippi and 126,689 reports of outages in Louisiana. 

Other states reporting outages were West Virginia, Virginia, Kentucky, North Carolina, South Carolina, Georgia and Texas.

The Trump administration has agreed to provide federal support to Tennessee, which was among some two dozen states that declared a state of emergency ahead of the storm. Republican Gov. Bill Lee (Tenn.) has also deployed the National Guard to assist in the storm response.

Nashville was hit particularly hard by the icy conditions.

“The combination of freezing rain and ice has impacted the NES service territory overnight, with trees being weighed down by ice causing them to snap and take down power lines. Right now, nearly 200,000 customers are currently without power, and at least 7 broken poles have been reported across our service area thus far,” Nashville Electric Service wrote in a post on X on Sunday, noting that outages could last for a day or longer.

“Our teams are fully staffed and already in the field assessing damage and beginning repairs, and contract crews have been called in to assist. Since yesterday, crews have been operating in continuous rotations, and they will remain on extended 14–16‑hour shifts to ensure we can continue responding to outages quickly,” the company added.

Duke Energy, which operates in seven states including Kentucky and the Carolinas, said most of its outages were reported in North Carolina. The company has said it deployed 18,000 restoration workers across the Carolinas.

“Ice is beginning to collect on trees and lines. One quarter inch can bring limbs down onto power lines, and one half inch can add enough weight to bring lines down themselves. Stay safe, stay prepared, and report outages right away,” the company wrote in a post on X.

Some 12 percent of homes in Mississippi were without power at the peak of the storm’s impact on Sunday.

“The situation is expected to get worse before it gets better,” Gov. Tate Reeves said on Sunday, warning that some places may be without power for days.

“We haven’t seen a storm of this magnitude in terms of ice since 1994,” he added. “This magnitude, this large an area in our state, and quite honestly, this much ice, we haven’t seen anything like this for 32 years.”
